item.text: I’ll read the local TaskLite context first and keep this to evaluation only, no file edits. I’m using the `wiki-explore` skill because this project includes a knowledge bundle that’s meant to inform proposals.

---
name: wiki-explore
description: Consult the project's saidwhen knowledge bundle (knowledge/) before planning, proposing, or asking the user anything. Use at the start of any feature evaluation, design discussion, or requirements question â€” and whenever a request might touch a recorded decision.
---

# wiki-explore

This project keeps decision provenance in an OKF bundle at `knowledge/`.
Apply these rules â€” verbatim from saidwhen read-first v0.1 (normative source:
`behaviors/read-first.md`; CI keeps this copy in sync):

<!-- saidwhen:behavior read-first v0.1 -->
## Before planning or asking anything

1. Read `knowledge/index.md`. Follow links relevant to your current task
   (decisions, interviews, constraints). Budget: read what's relevant, not
   the whole bundle.
2. **Never ask the human a question the wiki already answers.** If an
   interview or constraint answers it, cite the file and move on.
3. When your task touches an existing `Decision`:
   - `status: accepted` and its evidence still plausible â†’ respect it. Do not
     re-litigate. Cite it.
   - Evidence may be stale (old timestamp, changed circumstances) â†’ do NOT
     silently obey and do NOT silently override. Ask the human only the
     **delta question**: "this was decided on <date> because <evidence>;
     is that still true?"

## When the wiki is silent

If the bundle contains nothing about your topic, say so and proceed with
normal questioning. **Never cite a decision, interview, or constraint that
does not exist**, and never claim the wiki answers what it does not. Recorded
constraints that genuinely apply to the new topic still apply.
<!-- /saidwhen:behavior read-first -->

After exploring, if decisions crystallize, use the wiki-capture skill.

item.text: I’ve got the read-first rule loaded. Next I’m checking the knowledge index alongside the repo shape so the recommendation is grounded in recorded decisions and actual TaskLite code.

item.aggregated_output:
# TaskLite (example project)

A fictional lightweight task tracker for freelancers, used to demonstrate the
Provenance Convention. Browse `knowledge/` and `specs/` â€” every requirement
links to the decision that created it, and every decision links to the human
answer that justified it.

---
type: Decision
title: Magic-link authentication
description: TaskLite authenticates via emailed single-use magic links; no passwords, no OAuth.
status: accepted
timestamp: 2026-07-08T10:00:00Z
tags: [auth, security]
---

# Magic-link authentication

TaskLite authenticates users with single-use magic links emailed to them.
No passwords are stored; no third-party OAuth providers are integrated.

## Evidence

- [Interview 2026-07-08 with Ivan](../interviews/2026-07-08-auth-scope.md):
  users are external freelancers with no corporate identity provider, and the
  project is [solo-maintained](../constraints/solo-maintainer.md) â€” auth must
  be near-zero maintenance.

## Rejected

- **OAuth / "Sign in with Google" SSO** â€” rejected because
  [users have no corporate IdP](../constraints/external-users.md) and each
  OAuth provider adds credential rotation, console setup, and breakage surface
  a [solo maintainer](../constraints/solo-maintainer.md) cannot afford.
  Revisit if a significant user segment demands social login.
- **Passwords** â€” rejected: storage, reset flows, and breach liability are the
  highest-maintenance option.

---
type: Interview
title: Auth scoping with Ivan
description: Q&A that scoped TaskLite's authentication approach.
timestamp: 2026-07-08T09:30:00Z
tags: [auth]
---

# Auth scoping â€” 2026-07-08, with Ivan (owner)

**Q: Who are the users? Do they need SSO?**
A: "No. Users are external freelancers â€” individuals, not companies. There is
no corporate identity provider involved."

**Q: How much auth maintenance can the project absorb?**
A: "Basically none. I'm the only maintainer and I work on this a few hours a
week. Anything that can break at 3am is out."

**Q: Any compliance or security requirements?**
A: "Nothing formal. Just don't store passwords, I don't want the liability."

# Authentication

## Requirements

### AUTH-1: Magic-link login

The system MUST authenticate users via single-use magic links sent to their
email address. No passwords are stored.
<!-- why: ../../knowledge/decisions/magic-link-auth.md -->
([why](../../knowledge/decisions/magic-link-auth.md))

### AUTH-2: Session lifetime

Sessions MUST expire after 30 days of inactivity.
([why](../../knowledge/decisions/magic-link-auth.md))

---
type: Constraint
title: Users are external freelancers
description: TaskLite users are individual freelancers with no corporate identity provider.
timestamp: 2026-07-08T09:30:00Z
tags: [users, auth]
---

Users are individual freelancers, not employees of an organization. There is
no corporate identity provider (Google Workspace, Entra, Okta) to integrate
with. Source: [interview 2026-07-08](../interviews/2026-07-08-auth-scope.md).

---
type: Constraint
title: Solo maintainer
description: One person builds and operates TaskLite, a few hours per week.
timestamp: 2026-07-08T09:30:00Z
tags: [ops]
---

TaskLite is built and operated by one person working a few hours per week.
Every design choice must minimize operational surface. Source:
[interview 2026-07-08](../interviews/2026-07-08-auth-scope.md).

item.text: So far: no existing export decision, and the strongest applicable context is “solo-maintained, minimize operational surface.” I’m doing a quick targeted search for task/export terminology before I propose scope.

item.text:
1. RECOMMENDATION

Proceed, but scope it as a small self-service export: an authenticated user can download a CSV of their own tasks from the task list.

Proposed MVP: synchronous download, no background jobs, no emailed reports, no Google Sheets integration, no admin/org export. CSV should include a header row, stable column order, proper CSV quoting, UTF-8 encoding, and ISO date/time fields. Default export should likely honor the current task filters unless PM wants “all my tasks” explicitly.

Before implementation, add a new requirement/decision entry for task export so it follows the project’s provenance pattern. I did not modify any files.

2. PRIOR CONTEXT (what prior project context informed this, with paths)

[README.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/README.md:3): TaskLite is a lightweight task tracker, and requirements are expected to link back to decisions.

[knowledge/index.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/knowledge/index.md:10): TaskLite is for freelancers and solo-maintained.

[knowledge/constraints/solo-maintainer.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/knowledge/constraints/solo-maintainer.md:9): one person operates it a few hours per week, so export should minimize operational surface.

[knowledge/constraints/external-users.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/knowledge/constraints/external-users.md:9): users are individual freelancers, which points toward per-user export rather than organization/admin export.

[knowledge/decisions/magic-link-auth.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/knowledge/decisions/magic-link-auth.md:12) and [specs/auth/spec.md](E:/PROJEKTI/saidwhen/evidence/experiment/round4-skill-trigger/fixture-s2/specs/auth/spec.md:7): users authenticate via magic links, so CSV export should require an active authenticated session.

No existing CSV/export/task requirement was present in `knowledge/` or `specs/`.

3. QUESTIONS (what you would ask the PM/owner)

1. Should export include all of a user’s tasks, or only the tasks matching the current filters/search?
2. Which columns are required: title, status, due date, created date, completed date, priority, notes, tags, project/client?
3. Should completed/archived/deleted tasks be included?
4. Is this strictly “my tasks only,” or is there any future need for sharing/exporting client or workspace data?
5. Is CSV enough for now, or is the real user need backup, reporting, invoicing, or spreadsheet analysis?
